new_proven_results.statement: Support 9 is eliminated for 1/6 <= p <= 1/5 in the strict reduced regime.
new_proven_results.sketch: No losing set has size at least 5; losing pairs, triples, and 4-sets have matching/intersection restrictions, giving layer bounds 21, 56, 56 and a positive binomial-polynomial lower bound.

new_failed_attempts.approach: Purely local universal good-merge lemma.
new_failed_attempts.reason: Seven equal weights with p in (2/7,1/3) make every mergeable pair locally bad even though the instance is not a counterexample.

new_failed_attempts.approach: Absolute Berry-Esseen for a dominant head plus tiny regular tail.
new_failed_attempts.reason: The normal margin in the one-head formula decays in the far tail while the uniform Berry-Esseen error does not.

new_failed_attempts.approach: Use k-wise intersection alone after complementing the bad family.
new_failed_attempts.reason: General k-wise intersecting monotone families can have too-large high-biased measure; the missing ingredient must exploit the linear-threshold form F={w(A)>q} and coordinate/threshold constraints.

new_bottlenecks: Prove or refute the proposed biased-measure theorem for k-wise intersecting monotone linear-threshold families in the range q in [(k-1)/k,k/(k+1)].
Close strict reduced support n=8 for p in [1/5,1/3].
Turn regular-tail plus finite-head certificates into a uniform large-support proof.
kb_updates.frontier: Prove the complement-side k-wise-intersecting LTF measure bound, or else close support 8 and use it as a stepping stone for a finite-head/critical-index proof.

task_assignments.description: Exploit the complement high-bias formulation and k-wise intersecting LTF structure.
task_assignments.solver_prompt:
You are attacking the missing sufficiency for p <= 1/3. Work self-contained and try to prove a new structural theorem.

Key reduction. Put q=1-p. The desired statement B(p), namely Pr_p[sum_i w_i v_i >= p] >= p for all nonnegative weights summing to 1, is equivalent to the upper-tail statement U(q): for all such weights and iid Bernoulli(q) variables xi_i, Pr_q[sum_i w_i xi_i > q] <= q. Indeed xi_i=1-v_i turns the bad event sum w_i v_i < p into sum w_i xi_i > q.

In a strict reduced counterexample at p, the family F={A: w(A)>q} has mu_q(F)>q. Also, if k=floor(1/p), equivalently p in [1/(k+1),1/k], then F is k-wise intersecting: any k members of F have nonempty intersection. F is also an increasing linear-threshold family, and in the reduced regime all coordinate weights satisfy w_i < p = 1-q.

Main target theorem to prove or disprove: For every integer k>=3, every q in [(k-1)/k, k/(k+1)], and every nonnegative weight vector w_i summing to 1 with max_i w_i < 1-q, if F={A: w(A)>q} is k-wise intersecting, then mu_q(F) <= q. This theorem would prove B(p) on every interval p in [1/(k+1),1/k], hence the full base interval [1/6,1/3], and then the bootstrap gives all p<=1/3.

Suggested tools: Frankl's k-wise intersection theorem on uniform layers; p-biased/intersection theorems; shifting/compressions preserving the LTF structure; junta/stability ideas; layer-by-layer estimates that combine k-wise intersection with the fact that F is the upper level set of the same weights used in the threshold q. Be alert that k-wise intersection alone is not enough in general; you must use the weighted-threshold structure.

Deliverables: Either (1) a rigorous proof of the target theorem, even first for k=3 and q in [2/3,3/4]; or (2) a counterexample to the theorem; or (3) a strong partial theorem with exact constants and a clear explanation of the remaining obstruction. Include all computations and polynomial inequalities explicitly.

task_assignments.description: Close the next finite-support obstruction, especially support 8.
task_assignments.solver_prompt:
Build directly on the finite layer-counting and structural methods in the referenced tasks. Work in the strict reduced regime: 0 < w_i < p, sum_i w_i = 1, p in [1/6,1/3), and D={A: w(A)<=p} is the losing down-set. We already know supports up to 7 are eliminated throughout the base interval; support 8 is known only in the lowest range from earlier work, and support 9 is known for [1/6,1/5].

Main target: Prove that support n=8 cannot be a strict reduced counterexample for every p in [1/5,1/3). If possible, also extend support n=9 beyond [1/6,1/5].

Use any rigorous finite method: layer counts d_j=|D_j|; matching constraints; Erdos-Ko-Rado and Erdos-Gallai type bounds; the degree-sequence inequality from t5; blocker tradeoffs like the support-7 proof in t7; complement high-weight families; Kruskal-Katona/downset shadow constraints; or exact finite hypergraph classification. Do not rely only on crude independent upper bounds for each layer if they are too weak; seek tradeoffs between many losing pairs/triples and few losing larger sets.

Concrete guidance for n=8: split p into natural ranges such as [1/5,1/4], [1/4,2/7], [2/7,1/3]. For each range, determine which layers can be losing using the complement weight bound w(A)=1-w(A^c) and w_i<p. Then prove mu_p(D)<1-p by explicit polynomial bounds. If an extremal count pattern seems possible, analyze whether actual weights can realize it.

Deliverables: A self-contained proof for n=8 on as much of [1/5,1/3) as possible, with explicit layer bounds and polynomial checks; or a precise obstruction/hypergraph configuration showing why the current methods fail.

# Referenced Prior Solver Outputs

Each section starts with a metadata line that tells you whether the artifact has been verified by an independent verifier+refiner: a `verified ✓` block is the canonical, refined text and its claim/relaxation flags are authoritative; an `unverified self-report` block is a raw draft whose claim flags are the agent's own self-declaration and have NOT been checked. Examine the actual arguments — do not rely solely on summaries elsewhere in this prompt.

## Task t5_bootstrap_base_combinatorial — Exploit the bootstrap reduction and attack the base interval [1/6,1/3] by combinatorial and minimal-counterexample methods.
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
I did **not** close the whole base interval \([1/6,1/3]\) in this pass. I did get several clean new reductions/lemmas that shrink the possible counterexample space, especially in the lower two subintervals.

Below \(q=1-p\), \(S=\sum_i w_i v_i\), and in the strict reduced regime
\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1,\qquad D=\{A:w(A)\le p\}.
\]

---

## 1. \(k\)-colour bootstrap lemma

Let \(B(r)\) denote the non-strict assertion at parameter \(r\). If \(B(kp)\) holds for an integer \(k\) with \(kp\le1\), then \(B(p)\) holds.

**Proof.** Generate \(\eta_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(kp)\). Conditional on \(\eta_i=1\), give \(i\) a uniformly random colour in \([k]\). For each colour \(j\), the indicators \(\mathbf 1_{\{i\text{ has colour }j\}}\) are iid Bernoulli\((p)\). Let
\[
S_j=\sum_{i:\,\text{colour }j}w_i,\qquad T=\sum_{j=1}^k S_j.
\]
If \(T\ge kp\), then some \(S_j\ge p\). Hence
\[
k\,\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge \Pr[T\ge kp]\ge kp,
\]
so \(\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge p\). ∎

Thus the global problem is indeed reduced to the base interval \([1/6,1/3]\).

---

## 2. New layer-counting progress

### Proposition 2.1: support \(7\), lower two ranges

If \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p
\]
for all
\[
\frac16\le p\le \frac14.
\]

#### Proof

Let \(D_j=\{A\in D:|A|=j\}\), and write \(d_j=|D_j|\).

---

### Case 1: \(\frac16\le p\le\frac15\)

No losing triple exists: if \(A\in D_3\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1,
\]
a contradiction. Hence \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge3\).

Also the losing-pair graph has no two disjoint edges: two disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining three vertices would have total weight
\[
<2p+3p\le1.
\]
Thus \(d_2\le6\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+6p^2q^5.
\]
So
\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge 1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5.
\]
Set
\[
F_1(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_1'(p)=30p q^4-1>0
\]
on \([1/6,1/5]\), and
\[
F_1(1/6)=\frac{4505}{46656}>0.
\]
So \(F_1(p)>0\), proving the claim in this range.

---

### Case 2: \(\frac15\le p\le\frac14\)

No losing \(4\)-set exists, because if \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+3p\le1.
\]
So \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge4\).

The losing triples form an intersecting \(3\)-uniform family: two disjoint losing triples plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<2p+p<1.
\]
By Erdős–Ko–Rado,
\[
d_3\le \binom62=15.
\]

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\): three disjoint losing pairs plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<3p+p\le1.
\]
By the Erdős–Gallai matching bound, \(d_2\le11\); I use the weaker bound \(d_2\le12\). Hence
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4.
\]
Let
\[
F_2(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_2'(p)=18p q^5+15p^2q^4+60p^3q^3-1>0
\]
on \([1/5,1/4]\), and
\[
F_2(1/5)=\frac{1316}{78125}>0.
\]
Thus \(F_2(p)>0\). ∎

---

## 3. Support \(8\) in the lowest base range

### Proposition 3.1

If \(n=8\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac16\le p\le\frac15,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### Proof

No losing \(4\)-set exists: if \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1.
\]
Thus \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge4\).

The losing triples are intersecting, since two disjoint losing triples plus the remaining two vertices have total weight
\[
<2p+2p<1.
\]
Hence
\[
d_3\le \binom72=21.
\]

The losing-pair graph has no matching of size \(3\): three disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining two vertices have total weight
\[
<3p+2p\le1.
\]
By Erdős–Gallai,
\[
d_2\le13.
\]
Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^8+8pq^7+13p^2q^6+21p^3q^5.
\]
Let
\[
F_8(p)=1-q^8-8pq^7-13p^2q^6-21p^3q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_8'(p)=30p q^6+15p^2q^5+105p^3q^4-1>0
\]
on \([1/6,1/5]\), and
\[
F_8(1/6)=\frac{115305}{1679616}>0.
\]
So \(F_8(p)>0\), proving the claim. ∎

Consequently, any strict counterexample in the lowest base range \([1/6,1/5]\) must have support at least \(9\).

---

## 4. Support \(7\), upper base range: matching obstruction isolated

### Proposition 4.1

Let \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac14\le p\le\frac13.
\]
If the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### Proof

Every set of size at least \(5\) is winning, because its complement has size at most \(2\), so
\[
w(A)>1-2p\ge p
\]
with strictness coming from \(w_i<p\).

The losing triples are intersecting: two disjoint losing triples plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<2p+p\le1.
\]
Hence
\[
d_3\le15.
\]

The losing \(4\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting: if two losing \(4\)-sets met in only one vertex, their union would be all seven vertices, and the sum of their weights would be at least \(1\), but also at most \(2p\le2/3\), impossible. Equivalently, their complements form an intersecting \(3\)-family, so
\[
d_4\le15.
\]

By assumption and Erdős–Gallai, \(d_2\le12\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4+15p^4q^3.
\]
Let
\[
F_3(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-15p^4q^3-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_3(1/4)=\frac{231}{8192}>0,
\]
and a direct derivative check gives
\[
F_3'(p)=18p q^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2-1>0
\]
on \([1/4,1/3]\). Hence \(F_3(p)>0\). ∎

So a support-\(7\) counterexample in \([1/4,1/3]\), if one exists, must have **three disjoint losing pairs**.

---

## 5. Useful degree test for arbitrary-support layer attacks

Let \(\mathcal H\subseteq D_k\) be any losing \(k\)-uniform subfamily. Let \(d_i=\deg_{\mathcal H}(i)\), and sort the degrees:
\[
d_{(1)}\le d_{(2)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}.
\]
Since every \(A\in\mathcal H\) satisfies \(w(A)\le p\),
\[
\sum_i d_i w_i=\sum_{A\in\mathcal H}w(A)\le p|\mathcal H|.
\]
Let \(s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\) and \(\theta=1-sp\). Since \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), the left side is at least the LP minimum
\[
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\theta d_{(s+1)}.
\]
Therefore every losing layer must satisfy the necessary condition
\[
\boxed{
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\bigl(1-sp\bigr)d_{(s+1)}
\le p|\mathcal H|.
}
\]
This is especially useful in the base ranges:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
p\text{-range} & s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\\
\hline
[1/4,1/3] & 3\text{ or }4\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/5,1/4] & 4\text{ or }5\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/6,1/5] & 5\text{ or }6\text{ at the endpoint}
\end{array}
\]

This degree test rules out many extremal-looking layer configurations that raw matching bounds allow. For example, in \(n=7\), an intersecting \(3\)-family of size \(15\) is a star, with degree sequence \((5,5,5,5,5,5,15)\). For \(p<1/3\), the degree test forbids all \(15\) triples from being losing.

---

## 6. Minimal-counterexample merge lemma

This is a useful structural condition for any future perturbation argument.

Assume \(w\) is a strict counterexample of minimal support. Let \(x=w_i\le y=w_j\) with
\[
x+y<p.
\]
Let \(R=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j}w_\ell v_\ell\). Merge \(x,y\) into a single weight \(x+y\). By minimality, the merged instance is not a counterexample. Comparing the split and merged probabilities gives
\[
\Pr_{\mathrm{split}}[S>p]-\Pr_{\mathrm{merged}}[S>p]
=
pq\Big(
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
-
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\Big).
\]
Since the split instance is bad and the merged instance is good, necessarily
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
<
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
}
\]
Thus every mergeable pair in a minimal counterexample forces more residual mass in the lower window \((p-x-y,p-y]\) than in the upper window \((p-x,p]\). This is a strong anti-smoothing condition.

---

## 7. Current remaining obstruction

After combining the prior support-\(\le6\) work with the new lemmas above:

- In \([1/6,1/5]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(9\).
- In \([1/5,1/4]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(8\).
- In \([1/4,1/3]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(7\); if support is \(7\), its losing-pair graph must contain three disjoint losing pairs.

The main unresolved piece is still an arbitrary-support layer bound that controls the tradeoff between many losing pairs and the forced scarcity of losing triples and losing higher sets.

---

## Task t7_merge_minimal_counterexample — Attack the minimal-counterexample merge obstruction.
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
Let \(q=1-p\), and for a set \(A\) write \(w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i\). I use
\[
D=\{A:w(A)\le p\},\qquad D_k=D\cap {[n]\choose k}.
\]

## 1. The naive universal good-merge statement is false

The statement “for arbitrary reduced weights there is always a mergeable pair with
\[
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]\ge \Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\]
” is false.

Take \(p=0.3\), \(n=7\), and \(w_i=1/7\) for all \(i\). Every pair is mergeable, since \(2/7<p\). For any pair \(x=y=1/7\), the residual is \(R=K/7\), \(K\sim \mathrm{Bin}(5,p)\). Then
\[
(p-x,p]=(0.157\ldots,0.3]
\]
contains exactly the residual level \(K=2\), while
\[
(p-x-y,p-y]=(0.014\ldots,0.157\ldots]
\]
contains exactly the residual level \(K=1\). Hence
\[
\frac{\Pr[p-x<R\le p]}{\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]}
=\frac{\binom52p^2q^3}{\binom51pq^4}
=\frac{2p}{q}<1
\]
for every \(p<1/3\). Thus **all mergeable pairs are bad** in the merge sense. But this vector is not a counterexample: for \(p=0.3\),
\[
\Pr[S>p]=\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(7,0.3)\ge 3]\approx 0.353>0.3.
\]

So the missing ingredient cannot be a purely local merge statement. One must use the actual counterexample hypothesis \(\mu_p(D)>1-p\) quantitatively.

---

## 2. A useful conditional good-merge lemma for the two smallest weights

Let \(a\le b\) be the two smallest weights, with \(a+b<p\), and let \(R\) be the residual sum. Define
\[
L=\{T:p-a-b<w(T)\le p-b\},
\]
\[
U=\{A:p-a<w(A)\le p\}.
\]
The merge is good exactly when \(\mu_p(U)\ge \mu_p(L)\).

For \(T\in L\), define its losing upper shadow
\[
\Gamma(T)=\{k\notin T:\ w(T)+w_k\le p\}.
\]
Because \(a,b\) are the two smallest weights, every residual weight is at least \(b\). Thus if \(T\in L\) and \(k\in \Gamma(T)\), then
\[
w(T)+w_k>p-a-b+b=p-a
\]
and also \(w(T)+w_k\le p\), so
\[
T\cup\{k\}\in U.
\]

Let \(K\) be the maximum number of lower-shadow predecessors of any \(A\in U\), i.e.
\[
K=\max_{A\in U}|\{k\in A:A\setminus\{k\}\in L\}|.
\]
Then
\[
\mu_p(U)\ge \frac{p}{qK}\sum_{T\in L}|\Gamma(T)|\,\mu_p(T).
\]

Therefore, if
\[
\frac{\sum_{T\in L}|\Gamma(T)|\,\mu_p(T)}{\mu_p(L)}
\ge \frac{q}{p}K,
\]
then the pair \(a,b\) is a good merge.

Equivalently, in a minimal counterexample where every mergeable pair is bad, the two-smallest lower window must have unusually poor \(p\)-biased upper-shadow expansion:
\[
\frac{\sum_{T\in L}|\Gamma(T)|\,\mu_p(T)}{\mu_p(L)}
< \frac{q}{p}K.
\]
For \(p\le 1/3\), the factor \(q/p\ge 2\), so this says the lower window must be very close to maximal losing sets. This is a concrete structural obstruction that any future proof must exploit.

---

## 3. Support \(7\), upper interval closed

I can close the remaining support-\(7\) upper-interval case, including the previous obstruction with three disjoint losing pairs.

### Proposition

Let \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac14\le p<\frac13.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

Thus no strict reduced counterexample of support \(7\) exists in the upper base interval.

### Proof

Let \(W\) be the graph of **winning pairs**:
\[
ij\in W \iff w_i+w_j>p.
\]
Write \(m=|W|\). Then
\[
d_2=|D_2|=21-m.
\]

Because every \(w_i<p\), every set of size at least \(5\) is winning:
\[
|A|\ge 5
\implies w(A)=1-w(A^c)>1-2p>p.
\]
So only layers \(0,1,2,3,4\) matter.

#### Step 1: losing triples are intersecting

If \(A,B\in D_3\) were disjoint, and \(z\) were the remaining vertex, then
\[
1=w(A)+w(B)+w_z<p+p+p=3p\le 1,
\]
a contradiction. Hence \(D_3\) is intersecting, so by EKR,
\[
d_3\le 15.
\]

#### Step 2: complements of losing triples force winning-pair blockers

Let \(T\in D_3\), and let \(Q=T^c\), so \(|Q|=4\). If \(Q\) could be partitioned into two losing pairs, then
\[
1=w(T)+w(P_1)+w(P_2)\le 3p<1,
\]
impossible. Hence \(W[Q]\) must meet every perfect matching of \(K_4\). A subgraph of \(K_4\) meeting all three perfect matchings contains at least three edges; its minimal blockers are a triangle or a \(3\)-edge star.

This gives the following graph-count bounds for \(d_3\):

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
m & d_3\text{ bound}\\
\hline
m\le 2 & d_3=0\\
3\le m\le 4 & d_3\le 4\\
m=5 & d_3\le 10\\
m\ge 6 & d_3\le 15
\end{array}
\]

The \(m=5\) bound is maximized by a \(5\)-edge star, which gives \(\binom53=10\) four-sets whose winning-pair graph contains a \(3\)-star.

#### Step 3: losing four-sets are \(3\)-intersecting

If \(A,B\in D_4\) and \(|A\cap B|\le 2\), then one can choose disjoint triples \(A'\subset A\), \(B'\subset B\). Since every subset of a losing set is losing, this would give two disjoint losing triples, impossible. Therefore \(D_4\) is \(3\)-intersecting.

Equivalently, the complements of sets in \(D_4\) form a \(2\)-intersecting \(3\)-uniform family on \(7\) vertices. Hence
\[
d_4\le 5.
\]
Moreover, if \(d_3\le 4\), then \(d_4\le 1\), because one losing \(4\)-set already contributes its four \(3\)-subsets to \(D_3\), and two distinct \(4\)-sets would contribute at least seven triples.

#### Step 4: pair-sum feasibility

The sum of all pair weights is
\[
\sum_{i<j}(w_i+w_j)=6.
\]
Each losing pair has weight at most \(p\), and each winning pair has weight less than \(2p\). Hence
\[
6\le (21-m)p+2mp=(21+m)p.
\]
In particular, when \(m\le 2\),
\[
p\ge \frac6{23}.
\]

#### Step 5: polynomial check

For bounds
\[
d_2\le a,\qquad d_3\le b,\qquad d_4\le c,
\]
we have
\[
\mu_p(D)
\le q^7+7pq^6+a p^2q^5+b p^3q^4+c p^4q^3.
\]
Writing \(r=p/q\), this is
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7(1+7r+a r^2+b r^3+c r^4).
\]
Since
\[
1-p=q=q^7(1+r)^6,
\]
it is enough to prove
\[
(1+r)^6>1+7r+a r^2+b r^3+c r^4.
\]

The four cases are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c}
\text{case} & (a,b,c) & r\text{ range} & \text{positive factor}\\
\hline
m\le 2 & (21,0,0) & r\ge 6/17 &
-1-6r+20r^2+15r^3+6r^4+r^5\\
3\le m\le 4 & (18,4,1) & r\ge 1/3 &
-1-3r+16r^2+14r^3+6r^4+r^5\\
m=5 & (16,10,5) & r\ge 1/3 &
-1-r+10r^2+10r^3+6r^4+r^5\\
m\ge 6 & (15,15,5) & r\ge 1/3 &
-1+5r^2+10r^3+6r^4+r^5
\end{array}
\]

In each row,
\[
(1+r)^6-(1+7r+a r^2+b r^3+c r^4)=r\cdot h(r),
\]
and \(h(r)\) is increasing on the stated interval. At the left endpoints the values are respectively
\[
\frac{187075}{17^5},\qquad
\frac{91}{243},\qquad
\frac{55}{243},\qquad
\frac1{243},
\]
all positive. Hence \(\mu_p(D)<1-p\), so
\[
\Pr[S>p]=1-\mu_p(D)>p.
\]
This proves the proposition. ∎

At \(p=1/3\), the original non-strict statement \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\) is already covered by the cyclic \(p=1/k\) argument with \(k=3\). The strict \(>\) endpoint is a boundary issue and is not needed for \(B(1/3)\).

---

## 4. Consequences

Combining this with the prior support-\(7\) result for \([1/6,1/4]\), support \(7\) is now eliminated throughout the base interval \(1/6\le p<1/3\). Therefore any strict reduced counterexample with \(p<1/3\) has support at least \(8\).

The merge program remains open in arbitrary support, but the two key takeaways are:

1. **All local merge inequalities can hold in non-counterexamples**, so the counterexample condition \(\mu_p(D)>1-p\) is essential.
2. In a minimal counterexample, every mergeable pair must have a lower-window family with abnormally small biased upper-shadow expansion.

## Task t8_regular_critical_index — Develop a Berry-Esseen plus critical-index proof for large support.
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
Below is a **large-support / regularity progress report**. I did not close every nonregular configuration, but the complementary cases are now reduced to explicit finite-head certificates plus a critical-index decay lemma, and I also get one new small-support layer-counting extension.

Throughout, reduce to the strict case
\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1,\qquad p\in[1/6,1/3],
\]
since any \(w_i\ge p\) immediately gives \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\). Sort
\[
w_1\ge w_2\ge\cdots>0.
\]
For \(k\ge0\), write
\[
\alpha_k=\sum_{i\le k}w_i,\quad
B_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^2,\quad
C_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^3,\quad
\rho_k=\frac{C_k}{B_k^{3/2}},
\]
and
\[
\sigma_k^2=pqB_k,\qquad X_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i(v_i-p).
\]

Let
\[
K(p)=\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}.
\]
On \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\),
\[
K(p)\le K_*:=\frac{13}{3\sqrt5}<2.
\]

---

## 1. Finite-head / regular-tail Berry–Esseen certificate

For any fixed head size \(k\), condition on the head
\[
H_k=\sum_{i\le k}w_i v_i.
\]
Given a head set \(A\subseteq[k]\), the tail must satisfy
\[
X_k\ge p\alpha_k-w(A).
\]
Berry–Esseen gives
\[
\sup_x\left|\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_k}{\sigma_k}\le x\right]-\Phi(x)\right|
\le C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_k=:\varepsilon_k.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge
G_k-\varepsilon_k,
}
\]
where
\[
G_k=
\sum_{A\subseteq[k]}
p^{|A|}q^{k-|A|}
\overline\Phi\!\left(\frac{p\alpha_k-w(A)}{\sigma_k}\right),
\qquad \overline\Phi=1-\Phi.
\]

This is the main regular-tail certificate: if for some \(k\),
\[
G_k\ge p+C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_k,
\]
then \(B(p)\) holds.

---

## 2. A simple small-head corollary

Let \(C_{\rm BE}\le1\) for concreteness; otherwise replace constants by \(1/C_{\rm BE}\). Put
\[
\eta_0=\frac{1}{64C_{\rm BE}}.
\]
If for some \(k\),
\[
\rho_k\le \eta_0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\alpha_k\le \sigma_k,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]>p.
\]

Indeed, since \(H_k\ge0\),
\[
p\alpha_k-H_k\le p\alpha_k\le p\sigma_k\le \frac{\sigma_k}{3}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]
\ge \overline\Phi(1/3)-C_{\rm BE}K_*\eta_0
> \overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac1{32}.
\]
Numerically,
\[
\overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac13\approx0.0361>\frac1{32},
\]
so the right side is \(>p\) for every \(p\le1/3\).

Thus a genuinely regular tail after a head whose total mass is at most one tail standard deviation is enough.

---

## 3. One-dominant-head refinement

For \(k=1\), the finite-head certificate becomes especially clean. Let \(w=w_1\), \(\sigma=\sigma_1\), \(t=w/\sigma\). Then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]-p
\ge
q\overline\Phi(pt)-p\overline\Phi(qt)
-C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_1.
\]
Define
\[
M_p(t):=q\overline\Phi(pt)-p\overline\Phi(qt).
\]
Since \(q\ge p\) and \(pt\le qt\), one has \(M_p(t)>0\) for every finite \(t\). Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_1\le \frac{M_p(w_1/\sigma_1)}{C_{\rm BE}K(p)}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p.
}
\]

This handles many “one large atom plus regular tail” cases even when \(w_1\) is much larger than \(\sigma_1\). The remaining difficulty is that \(M_p(t)\) decays in the far tail, so absolute Berry–Esseen is not enough when \(t\) is very large; one would need relative moderate-deviation estimates there.

---

## 4. Critical-index decay for nonregular tails

Fix \(\tau>0\). If a tail is not \(\tau\)-regular, i.e.
\[
\rho_k>\tau,
\]
then, because \(C_k\le w_{k+1}B_k\),
\[
w_{k+1}>\tau\sqrt{B_k}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
B_{k+1}=B_k-w_{k+1}^2<(1-\tau^2)B_k.
\]

Thus before the first \(\tau\)-regular tail, the tail variance decays geometrically:
\[
B_{k+r}\le (1-\tau^2)^r B_k.
\]

Moreover, if no \(\tau\)-regular tail appears after \(K\), then the whole remaining tail mass satisfies
\[
\boxed{
\beta_K:=\sum_{i>K}w_i
\le
\frac{\tau}{1-\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}\sqrt{B_K}
<\frac{2}{\tau}\sqrt{B_K}.
}
\]

Proof sketch: put \(u_j=\sqrt{B_j}\). Since \(u_{j+1}\le \sqrt{1-\tau^2}\,u_j\),
\[
w_{j+1}=\sqrt{u_j^2-u_{j+1}^2}
\le
\sqrt{\frac{1+\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}{1-\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}}\,(u_j-u_{j+1}),
\]
and summing telescopes.

So a long nonregular segment forces geometric decay; either a regular tail eventually appears, or the remaining tail has small total mass and the problem becomes an explicit finite-head problem.

---

## 5. Deterministic tail-filling reduction

Suppose after a head of size \(K\), the remaining raw tail
\[
T_K=\sum_{i>K}w_i v_i
\]
has total mass \(\beta_K\). We always know
\[
0\le T_K\le \beta_K,\qquad \mathbb E T_K=p\beta_K.
\]
For any residual threshold \(r\), the sharp mean/range lower bound is
\[
\Pr[T_K\ge r]\ge \psi_{\beta_K}(r),
\]
where
\[
\psi_\beta(r)=
\begin{cases}
1, & r\le0,\\[2mm]
\dfrac{p\beta-r}{\beta-r}, & 0<r\le p\beta,\\[3mm]
0, & r>p\beta.
\end{cases}
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge
\sum_{A\subseteq[K]}
p^{|A|}q^{K-|A|}
\psi_{\beta_K}\bigl(p-w(A)\bigr).
}
\]

This is useful in the no-regular-tail case: critical-index decay gives an explicit small \(\beta_K\), and the last display reduces the problem to a finite weighted layer-counting check on the head.

---

## 6. New layer-counting extension: support \(9\) in the lowest base range

I can extend the previous support-\(8\) result one step.

### Proposition

If \(n=9\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac16\le p\le \frac15,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

### Proof

Let \(D_j\) be the losing \(j\)-sets:
\[
D_j=\{A\subseteq[9]: |A|=j,\ w(A)\le p\}.
\]

No losing set of size at least \(5\) exists, because a losing \(5\)-set plus the four remaining weights would have total
\[
< p+4p\le1,
\]
contradicting \(\sum_iw_i=1\).

For losing pairs, four disjoint losing pairs plus the ninth vertex would have total
\[
<4p+p\le1,
\]
so the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(3\). By Erdős–Gallai,
\[
|D_2|\le21.
\]

For losing triples, three disjoint losing triples would cover all vertices and have total at most
\[
3p<1,
\]
impossible. Thus the losing triple family has matching number at most \(2\). By the Erdős matching theorem for \(3\)-graphs on \(9\) vertices,
\[
|D_3|\le56.
\]

For losing \(4\)-sets, two disjoint losing \(4\)-sets plus the last vertex would have total
\[
<2p+p<1,
\]
impossible, so \(D_4\) is intersecting. By Erdős–Ko–Rado,
\[
|D_4|\le \binom83=56.
\]

Therefore
\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le
q^9+9pq^8+21p^2q^7+56p^3q^6+56p^4q^5.
\]
Define
\[
F_9(p)=
1-q^9-9pq^8-21p^2q^7-56p^3q^6-56p^4q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_9(1/6)=\frac{238705}{10077696}>0.
\]
Also
\[
F_9'(p)=
30pq^7-21p^2q^6+112p^3q^5+280p^4q^4-1.
\]
On \([1/6,1/5]\),
\[
30pq^7-21p^2q^6
=
pq^6(30-51p)
\ge
\frac15\left(\frac45\right)^6\cdot\frac{99}{5}
>1,
\]
so \(F_9'(p)>0\). Hence \(F_9(p)>0\) throughout \([1/6,1/5]\), proving the claim.

---

## Current state

The regular side is now quite explicit:

- If the whole vector is regular, Berry–Esseen proves \(B(p)\).
- If a regular tail remains after a small head, the small-head criterion proves \(B(p)\).
- If there is one dominant head atom and a regular tail, the one-head margin criterion often proves \(B(p)\).
- If no regular tail appears, the critical-index lemma forces geometric decay and reduces the problem to a finite-head layer-counting certificate with an explicit tail-filling function.

What remains is to close the finite-head certificates uniformly when the first regular tail occurs only after a large head, or when the no-regular-tail reduction leaves a large but geometrically decaying head. Absolute Berry–Esseen is too crude in the extreme-tail one-head regime; a relative moderate-deviation estimate for the regular tail looks like the next natural improvement.

Build directly on the finite layer-counting and structural methods in the referenced tasks. Work in the strict reduced regime: 0 < w_i < p, sum_i w_i = 1, p in [1/6,1/3), and D={A: w(A)<=p} is the losing down-set. We already know supports up to 7 are eliminated throughout the base interval; support 8 is known only in the lowest range from earlier work, and support 9 is known for [1/6,1/5].

Main target: Prove that support n=8 cannot be a strict reduced counterexample for every p in [1/5,1/3). If possible, also extend support n=9 beyond [1/6,1/5].

Use any rigorous finite method: layer counts d_j=|D_j|; matching constraints; Erdos-Ko-Rado and Erdos-Gallai type bounds; the degree-sequence inequality from t5; blocker tradeoffs like the support-7 proof in t7; complement high-weight families; Kruskal-Katona/downset shadow constraints; or exact finite hypergraph classification. Do not rely only on crude independent upper bounds for each layer if they are too weak; seek tradeoffs between many losing pairs/triples and few losing larger sets.

Concrete guidance for n=8: split p into natural ranges such as [1/5,1/4], [1/4,2/7], [2/7,1/3]. For each range, determine which layers can be losing using the complement weight bound w(A)=1-w(A^c) and w_i<p. Then prove mu_p(D)<1-p by explicit polynomial bounds. If an extremal count pattern seems possible, analyze whether actual weights can realize it.

Deliverables: A self-contained proof for n=8 on as much of [1/5,1/3) as possible, with explicit layer bounds and polynomial checks; or a precise obstruction/hypergraph configuration showing why the current methods fail.

writeup_tasks.statement: Complement upper-tail and k-wise-intersection reduction for strict counterexamples to the Bernoulli weighted-sum inequality.
writeup_tasks.writeup_agent_prompt:
Produce a fully self-contained mathematical note proving the following partial structural reduction.

Claim. Let m be a positive integer, let w_1,...,w_m be nonnegative real numbers with sum_i w_i=1, and let p in (0,1) with q=1-p. For iid Bernoulli(p) variables v_i and iid Bernoulli(q) variables xi_i, the inequality Pr[sum_i w_i v_i >= p] >= p is equivalent, for the same weight vector, to Pr[sum_i w_i xi_i > q] <= q. Moreover, if D={A subset [m]: sum_{i in A} w_i < p} and F={B subset [m]: sum_{i in B} w_i > q}, then complementation maps D to F and mu_p(D)=mu_q(F). Finally, for every integer r with r p <= 1, the family F is r-wise intersecting: any r members of F have nonempty intersection. In particular, if p in [1/(k+1),1/k], then F is k-wise intersecting.

The proof must define mu_t for the t-biased measure on the discrete cube, define r-wise intersecting, and include every quantifier and hypothesis. It must not refer to any shared knowledge base or previous conversation. Conclude with a short paragraph explaining why this reduction is useful for strict counterexamples: a failure of the original inequality gives mu_q(F)>q for an increasing linear-threshold family F that is k-wise intersecting on the appropriate reciprocal interval.

plan.reasoning: Round 5 closed the concrete support-8 obstruction: combined with earlier work, no strict reduced counterexample with 8 positive weights remains in the base interval [1/6,1/3). The complement-side solver proved useful structural facts, especially the support-excess lemma, but also showed that the k-wise-intersection property is automatic and therefore cannot by itself prove the theorem. Also note that t9's stated first k=3,n=6 obstruction is already covered by earlier support-6 results; the live finite-support frontier is support 9, especially p in [1/5,1/3). The next round should therefore run one ambitious task on the high-bias LTF theorem beyond Frankl-Tokushige, and one concrete task on support 9 / robust finite-head certificates for the critical-index route.
plan.strategic_note: Support 8 is closed; do not spend more solver effort there except for write-up. The two best remaining paths are: prove the complement high-bias LTF bound using more than automatic k-wise intersection, or turn support-8/9 finite certificates into a critical-index large-support proof. Support 9 is the next finite obstruction and may also be exactly what the finite-head Berry-Esseen strategy needs.

new_proven_results.statement: In the strict reduced regime with support n=8, Pr[sum_i w_i v_i > p] > p for every p in [1/5,1/3); combined with earlier work this eliminates support 8 throughout [1/6,1/3).
new_proven_results.sketch: For [1/5,1/4), losing sets have rank at most 4, matching and EKR bounds give d2<=21 and d3<=21, and Kruskal-Katona improves d4<=15. For [1/4,1/3), complements of high losing layers are 2-intersecting, losing triples are controlled by Kneser supersaturation in terms of winning pairs, and additional high-pair/blocker refinements close the remaining polynomial gaps.

new_proven_results.statement: For p<=1/k, the complement family F={A: w(A)>1-p} is automatically k-wise intersecting, so k-wise intersection alone adds no information beyond the weighted lower-tail setup.
new_proven_results.sketch: If k complements B_j all have w(B_j)<p, then w(union B_j)<kp<=1, so they cannot cover the positive support; equivalently the original high-weight sets have common intersection.

new_proven_results.statement: Support-excess lemma: if p in [1/(k+1),1/k], support size n=k+s, and B_1,...,B_t are disjoint bad sets of sizes r_j, then sum_j (r_j-1)<s.
new_proven_results.sketch: The union of the bad sets has weight <tp, so the complement has weight >1-tp. If sum_j(r_j-1)>=s, then the complement has at most k-t coordinates, each of weight <p, contradicting 1-tp>=(k-t)p.

new_proven_results.statement: For p in [1/(k+1),1/k], the desired inequality holds in the reduced regime whenever the positive support size is at most k+2.
new_proven_results.sketch: The support-excess lemma forces rank at most 2 and bad-pair matching number at most 1. Counting gives mu_p(G)<=q^k(1+kp)<=q.

new_proven_results.statement: Literature endpoint: Frankl-Tokushige gives mu_theta(F)<=theta for every k-wise intersecting family F when theta<=(k-1)/k; this explains the reciprocal endpoints but does not cover the needed high-bias interval theta in [(k-1)/k,k/(k+1)].
new_proven_results.sketch: This theorem reaches q=(k-1)/k, corresponding to p=1/k, but our complement formulation for p in [1/(k+1),1/k] requires q=1-p above that endpoint, where general k-wise intersecting families can be too large.

new_failed_attempts.approach: Use k-wise intersection alone on the complement family.
new_failed_attempts.reason: The property is automatic for threshold families with p<=1/k and is false as a sufficient measure bound above the Frankl-Tokushige endpoint for general k-wise intersecting families.

new_failed_attempts.approach: Crude independent layer bounds for support 8 above p=1/4.
new_failed_attempts.reason: Bounds on d2,d3,d4,d5 separately are too weak; the successful proof needs tradeoffs involving winning pairs, blockers, high-pair graphs, and Kneser supersaturation.

new_failed_attempts.approach: Direct extension of the support-8 layer proof to support 9.
new_failed_attempts.reason: The n=9 middle and upper ranges introduce losing 5-sets and much larger matching-number-two triple families, so the n=8 estimates do not transfer without new tradeoffs.

new_bottlenecks: Prove the high-bias complement LTF theorem: if q=1-p>=2/3 and F={A:w(A)>q}, then mu_q(F)<=q.
Close support 9 for p in [1/5,1/3), or identify the exact extremal layer profiles blocking the support-8 methods.
Convert finite-support certificates into a robust finite-head lemma usable in the Berry-Esseen/critical-index proof for arbitrary support.
kb_updates.frontier: Either prove the complement high-bias LTF measure bound beyond the Frankl-Tokushige endpoint, or close support 9 and use it as the finite-head input for a large-support critical-index proof.

Rank-two theorem: target holds for support size at most \(k+2\) ### Theorem Let \(k\ge3\), \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), \(q=1-p\), and let \(w_i\in(0,p)\) sum to \(1\). If the positive support size satisfies \[ n\le k+2, \] then \[ \mu_q(F)=\mu_p(G)\le q. \] More generally, the same conclusion holds whenever \(G\) has no bad set of size at least \(3\), and the graph of bad pairs \[ E_2=\{\{i,j\}: w_i+w_j1/p\), so \(n\ge k+1\). #### Case 1: \(n=k+1\) For any two coordinates \(i,j\), the other \(k-1\) coordinates have total weight \(<(k-1)p\). Hence \[ w_i+w_j>1-(k-1)p\ge p. \] So no pair is bad. Thus the only bad sets are \(\varnothing\) and the \(n=k+1\) singletons. Therefore \[ \mu_p(G) = q^{k+1}+(k+1)pq^k = q^k(q+(k+1)p) = q^k(1+kp). \] #### Case 2: \(n\ge k+2\), rank \(\le2\), bad-pair matching number \(\le1\) The graph \(E_2\) has at most \(n-1\) edges: a graph with no two disjoint edges is either a star or a triangle, and \(n\ge5\). Therefore \[ \mu_p(G) \le q^n+npq^{n-1}+(n-1)p^2q^{n-2}. \] The bracket simplifies exactly: \[ q^n+npq^{n-1}+(n-1)p^2q^{n-2} = q^{n-2}\bigl(q^2+npq+(n-1)p^2\bigr) = q^{n-2}\bigl(1+(n-2)p\bigr). \] The function \[ h_n:=q^{n-2}(1+(n-2)p) \] is decreasing in \(n\), because \[ h_n-h_{n+1} = q^{n-2}(n-1)p^2>0. \] Hence for \(n\ge k+2\), \[ \mu_p(G)\le h_{k+2}=q^k(1+kp). \] So in all cases, \[ \mu_p(G)\le q^k(1+kp). \] It remains to check \[ q^k(1+kp)\le q. \] Equivalently, \[ (1-p)^{k-1}(1+kp)\le 1. \] Let \[ R_k(p)=(1-p)^{k-1}(1+kp). \] Then \[ R_k'(p) =(1-p)^{k-2}(1-k^2p). \] On \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\) and \(k\ge3\), we have \(1-k^2p<0\), so \(R_k\) is decreasing. Thus its maximum occurs at \(p=1/(k+1)\). There, \[ R_k\!\left(\frac1{k+1}\right) = \left(\frac{k}{k+1}\right)^{k-1}\frac{2k+1}{k+1}. \] It is enough to prove \[ \left(1+\frac1k\right)^{k-1} \ge \frac{2k+1}{k+1}. \] By the binomial expansion, \[ \left(1+\frac1k\right)^{k-1} \ge 1+\frac{k-1}{k} +\frac{(k-1)(k-2)}{2k^2}. \] Subtracting \((2k+1)/(k+1)=2-1/(k+1)\), the difference is \[ -\frac1k+\frac1{k+1} +\frac{(k-1)(k-2)}{2k^2} = \frac{(k-1)(k-2)(k+1)-2k}{2k^2(k+1)}\ge0 \] for every \(k\ge3\). Hence \(q^k(1+kp)\le q\). ∎ --- ## 4. Explicit \(k=3\) consequence For \(k=3\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), \(q=1-p\). [verified ✓ | is_relaxation: True | problem_solved (verifier-canonical, self-contained): The proof establishes the claimed partial structural reduction: for every positive integer \(m\), every nonnegative weight vector \((w_1,\dots,w_m)\) with total weight \(1\), every \(p\in(0,1)\), \(q=1-p\), independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\), and independent Bernoulli\((q)\) variables \(\xi_i\), the inequality \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p \] is equivalent, for the same weights, to \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i\xi_i>q\right]\le q. \] With \[ \mathcal D=\{A\subseteq[m]:w(A)q\}, \] complementation maps \(\mathcal D\) bijectively onto \(\mathcal F\), and \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)=\mu_q(\mathcal F)\). Also, for every positive integer \(r\) with \(rp\le1\), \(\mathcal F\) is \(r\)-wise intersecting; in particular, if \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), then \(\mathcal F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting. The proof also correctly notes that \(\mathcal F\) is an increasing linear-threshold family. It does not classify all \(p\) for which the original inequality holds universally.] Source: final_solutions.jsonl — verified+refined text, the artifact that actually survived the verify+refine loop. ## Original Problem Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let $v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that \[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\] ## Problem Solved The result proved below is the following **partial structural reduction**. Let \(m\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), let \([m]=\{1,\dots,m\}\), and let \(2^{[m]}\) denote the set of all subsets of \([m]\). Let \(w_1,\dots,w_m\in[0,\infty)\) satisfy \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1\), and for every \(S\subseteq[m]\) define \[ w(S)=\sum_{i\in S}w_i, \] with the empty sum equal to \(0\). Let \(p\in(0,1)\), and set \(q=1-p\). A Bernoulli\((t)\) random variable is a \(\{0,1\}\)-valued random variable equal to \(1\) with probability \(t\). Let \(v_1,\dots,v_m\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, and let \(\xi_1,\dots,\xi_m\) be independent Bernoulli\((q)\) random variables. For \(t\in[0,1]\) and \(\mathcal A\subseteq2^{[m]}\), define the \(t\)-biased measure on the discrete cube by \[ \mu_t(\mathcal A)=\sum_{S\in\mathcal A} t^{|S|}(1-t)^{m-|S|}, \] with the usual endpoint convention \(0^0=1\). Define \[ \mathcal D=\{A\subseteq[m]:w(A)q\}. \] For a positive integer \(r\), a family \(\mathcal H\subseteq2^{[m]}\) is called \(r\)-wise intersecting if, for every \(H_1,\dots,H_r\in\mathcal H\), not necessarily distinct, \[ H_1\cap\cdots\cap H_r\neq\varnothing. \] Then the following statements hold. 1. For the same fixed weight vector \(w_1,\dots,w_m\), \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i \xi_i>q\right]\le q. \] 2. The complementation map \(A\mapsto [m]\setminus A\) is a bijection from \(\mathcal D\) onto \(\mathcal F\), and \[ \mu_p(\mathcal D)=\mu_q(\mathcal F). \] 3. For every positive integer \(r\) satisfying \(rp\le1\), the family \(\mathcal F\) is \(r\)-wise intersecting. In particular, for every \(k\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), if \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), then \(\mathcal F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting. This differs from the original problem because it does not classify all probabilities \(p\) for which the original inequality holds universally. It proves a reduction and structural consequence for each fixed admissible weight vector and each fixed \(p\in(0,1)\). ## Proof Let \[ V=\{i\in[m]:v_i=1\}. \] Because the \(v_i\) are independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, for every \(A\subseteq[m]\), \[ \Pr[V=A]=p^{|A|}q^{m-|A|}. \] Thus \(V\) has law \(\mu_p\). Similarly, if \[ \Xi=\{i\in[m]:\xi_i=1\}, \] then \(\Xi\) has law \(\mu_q\), since \[ \Pr[\Xi=B]=q^{|B|}p^{m-|B|} \] for every \(B\subseteq[m]\). First prove the complementation statement. For every \(A\subseteq[m]\), \[ w([m]\setminus A)=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i-w(A)=1-w(A). \] Therefore \[ A\in\mathcal D \iff w(A)

1-p=q \iff w([m]\setminus A)>q \iff [m]\setminus A\in\mathcal F. \] Thus complementation maps \(\mathcal D\) bijectively onto \(\mathcal F\). Using this bijection, \[ \mu_p(\mathcal D) =\sum_{A\in\mathcal D}p^{|A|}q^{m-|A|} =\sum_{B\in\mathcal F}p^{m-|B|}q^{|B|} =\sum_{B\in\mathcal F}q^{|B|}(1-q)^{m-|B|} =\mu_q(\mathcal F), \] because \(1-q=p\). Now, \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right] =\Pr[w(V)\ge p] =1-\Pr[w(V)q\right] =\Pr[w(\Xi)>q] =\mu_q(\mathcal F). \] Since \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)=\mu_q(\mathcal F)\), we get \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p \iff 1-\mu_p(\mathcal D)\ge p \iff \mu_p(\mathcal D)\le q \iff \mu_q(\mathcal F)\le q \iff \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i\xi_i>q\right]\le q. \] This proves the equivalence. It remains to prove the \(r\)-wise intersection property. Let \(r\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\) satisfy \(rp\le1\). Suppose, toward a contradiction, that there exist \(B_1,\dots,B_r\in\mathcal F\) such that \[ B_1\cap\cdots\cap B_r=\varnothing. \] For each \(i\in[m]\), the element \(i\) is missing from at least one of the sets \(B_1,\dots,B_r\). Hence \[ \#\{j\in\{1,\dots,r\}:i\in B_j\}\le r-1. \] Therefore \[ \sum_{j=1}^r w(B_j) = \sum_{j=1}^r\sum_{i\in B_j}w_i = \sum_{i=1}^m w_i\,\#\{j:i\in B_j\} \le (r-1)\sum_{i=1}^m w_i =r-1. \] On the other hand, since every \(B_j\in\mathcal F\), \[ w(B_j)>q=1-p \] for every \(j\), and so \[ \sum_{j=1}^r w(B_j)>r(1-p)=r-rp\ge r-1, \] because \(rp\le1\). This contradicts the previous inequality. Hence every \(r\) members of \(\mathcal F\) have nonempty intersection, so \(\mathcal F\) is \(r\)-wise intersecting. Finally, if \(k\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\) and \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), then \(kp\le1\). Applying the preceding result with \(r=k\), the family \(\mathcal F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting. Because all weights are nonnegative, \(\mathcal F\) is also increasing: if \(B\in\mathcal F\) and \(B\subseteq C\subseteq[m]\), then \(w(C)\ge w(B)>q\), so \(C\in\mathcal F\). Thus \(\mathcal F\) is an increasing linear-threshold family, namely membership is determined by the strict linear inequality \(w(B)>q\). This reduction is useful for strict counterexamples as follows. A strict failure of the original inequality for fixed \(p\) and weights means \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]q\). Therefore, on the reciprocal interval \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), any strict counterexample produces an increasing linear-threshold family \(\mathcal F\) whose \(q\)-biased measure exceeds \(q\) and which is \(k\)-wise intersecting. ## Task t2_strict_threshold_minimal — Try to prove strict-threshold lemma for p<1/3 via threshold-family/minimal-counterexample analysis [unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let $v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that \[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]] Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established. ## Status I did **not** close the full strict lemma in this pass. I did get a clean reduction that makes the remaining obstruction quite precise: **continuous crossings are impossible**, so any strict counterexample must already occur immediately after a subset-sum threshold is crossed. I also proved the strict lemma for support size at most 5 and recorded two useful obstructions to natural proof attempts. --- ## 1. Basic reductions Let \[ X=w(S)=\sum_i w_i v_i,\qquad v_i\sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(p), \] independently, with \(w_i\ge 0\), \(\sum_i w_i=1\). Hence every pair has weight \(>p\). Therefore \[ \{w(S)>p\}=\{|S|\ge 2\}. \] So \[ \Pr[w(S)>p] =1-(1-p)^4-4p(1-p)^3 =6p^2-8p^3+3p^4. \] For \(p\in[1/4,1/3)\), \[ 6p^2-8p^3+3p^4-p =p(3p^3-8p^2+6p-1)>0. \] Thus the strict inequality holds. --- ### Case \(n=5\) If \(p<1/4\), the same argument shows every pair has weight \(>p\), because a nonwinning pair plus the complementary triple would have total at most \[ p+3p=4p<1. \] Hence \[ \Pr[w(S)>p]\ge \Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(5,p)\ge 2]>p. \] If \(p\ge 1/4\), consider the graph \(G\) of nonwinning pairs: \[ ij\in G\iff w_i+w_j\le p. \] Two disjoint nonwinning pairs cannot exist, because together with the fifth weight they would have total at most \[ p+p+p=3p<1. \] So \(G\) is an intersecting graph on five vertices, hence has at most \(4\) edges. Therefore at least \(6\) pairs are winning. The event \(\{w(S)>p\}\) contains: - every selected set of size at least \(3\), and - every selected set which is exactly a winning pair. Thus \[ \Pr[w(S)>p] \ge 6p^2(1-p)^3+\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(5,p)\ge 3]. \] A direct expansion gives \[ 6p^2(1-p)^3+10p^3(1-p)^2+5p^4(1-p)+p^5 =6p^2-8p^3+3p^4. \] As in the \(n=4\) case, this is \(>p\) for \(p\in[1/4,1/3)\). ∎ So any strict counterexample must have support size at least \(6\). --- ## 6. Reciprocal anchor: non-strict statement at \(p=1/k\) For completeness, here is the clean cyclic proof of the original non-strict statement when \(p=1/k\). Let \(p=1/k\). Represent each Bernoulli variable as \[ v_i=\mathbf 1_{\{Z_i=0\}}, \] where \(Z_i\) is uniform on \(\mathbb Z/k\mathbb Z\). Partition the product space into diagonal cyclic orbits: \[ (z_1,\dots,z_n)\mapsto (z_1+c,\dots,z_n+c). \] Each orbit has size \(k\). Along any orbit, each coordinate contributes \(1\) exactly once, so \[ \sum_{c=0}^{k-1} w(S_c)=\sum_i w_i=1. \] Hence the average value along the orbit is \(1/k=p\). Therefore at least one point in each orbit satisfies \[ w(S_c)\ge p. \] Thus \[ \Pr[w(S)\ge p]\ge \frac1k=p. \] This proves the original non-strict statement at reciprocal parameters \(p=1/k\). --- ## 7. Why the obvious cyclic extension fails For rational \(p=a/b\), one might try the same orbit argument with a success interval of length \(a\) in \(\mathbb Z/b\mathbb Z\). So the reciprocal cyclic proof does not extend orbitwise to general rational \(p\). --- ## 8. I was able to close **support \(n=8\)** throughout the requested base range:

\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^8w_i=1,\qquad \frac15\le p<\frac13.
\]

Equivalently, for \(D=\{A\subseteq[8]:w(A)\le p\}\),

\[
\mu_p(D)<1-p,
\]

so

\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]

I did **not** push \(n=9\) beyond the already known \([1/6,1/5]\) range in this pass.

---

# Theorem: support \(8\) is eliminated on \([1/5,1/3)\)

Let \(q=1-p\), \(r=p/q\), and \(d_j=|D_j|\). Since

\[
\mu_p(D)=q^8\sum_{j=0}^8 d_j r^j
\]

and

\[
1-p=q=q^8(1+r)^7,
\]

it suffices to prove

\[
\sum_j d_j r^j<(1+r)^7.
\]

---

## 1. Range \([1/5,1/4)\)

For \(p<1/4\), no losing set of size \(5\) or more exists. Also:

- four disjoint losing pairs would have total weight \(<1\), so the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(3\), hence by Erdős–Gallai
  \[
  d_2\le 21;
  \]
- two disjoint losing triples plus the remaining two vertices have total \(<4p\le1\), so \(D_3\) is intersecting, hence
  \[
  d_3\le \binom72=21;
  \]
- the lower shadow of \(D_4\) lies inside \(D_3\). By Kruskal–Katona, if \(d_4\ge16\), then \(|\partial D_4|\ge23>21\). Hence
  \[
  d_4\le15.
  \]

Thus

\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^8(1+8r+21r^2+21r^3+15r^4).
\]

But for \(r\in[1/4,1/3)\),

\[
(1+r)^7-(1+8r+21r^2+21r^3+15r^4)
=
r\bigl(-1+14r^2+20r^3+21r^4+7r^5+r^6\bigr)>0.
\]

So \(\mu_p(D)<q\).

---

## 2. Structural tools for \([1/4,1/3)\)

Let \(W\) be the graph of winning pairs,

\[
ij\in W \iff w_i+w_j>p,
\]

and put \(m=|W|\), so

\[
d_2=28-m.
\]

### High-layer complement bounds

If \(A\in D_4\), then \(A^c\) is a \(4\)-set of weight at least \(q\). If two such complements met in at most one vertex, their weights would sum to at most \(1+p\), contradicting \(2q>1+p\) for \(p<1/3\). Hence the complements of \(D_4\) form a \(2\)-intersecting \(4\)-family on \(8\) vertices. By the \(t=2\) Erdős–Ko–Rado/Frankl bound,

\[
d_4\le17.
\]

Similarly, complements of losing \(5\)-sets form a \(2\)-intersecting \(3\)-family, so

\[
d_5\le6.
\]

Also, every losing \(4\)-set complement must be a \(W\)-blocker: \(W\) must meet every perfect matching of that \(4\)-set. For small \(m\), direct graph checking gives

\[
m\le2\Rightarrow d_4=0,\qquad
m=3,4\Rightarrow d_4\le5,\qquad
m=5\Rightarrow d_4\le10.
\]

### Losing-triple bound

Let \(H=D_3\), \(h=d_3\). If \(T,U\in H\) are disjoint, then the complementary pair \(e=[8]\setminus(T\cup U)\) must be winning; otherwise \(w(T)+w(U)+w(e)\le3p<1\). For each winning edge \(e\), there are exactly \(10\) unordered partitions of the remaining \(6\) vertices into two triples. Hence the number \(I(H)\) of disjoint pairs in \(H\) satisfies

\[
I(H)\le10m.
\]

The Kneser graph \(KG(8,3)\) has \(56\) vertices, degree \(10\), and least eigenvalue \(-6\), so Hoffman supersaturation gives

\[
I(H)\ge \frac{h(h-21)}7.
\]

Therefore

\[
h\le H_m:=\left\lfloor \frac{21+\sqrt{441+280m}}2\right\rfloor.
\]

We also use a weight-deficiency bound. For a losing pair \(ij\), set

\[
\delta_{ij}=p-w_i-w_j\ge0.
\]

For every losing triple \(T\),

\[
\sum_{ij\subset T}\delta_{ij}=3p-2w(T)\ge p.
\]

Each losing pair lies in at most \(6\) triples, and

\[
\sum_{ij\in D_2}\delta_{ij}
\le ((28+m)p-7).
\]

Thus

\[
d_3\le 6\left(28+m-\frac7p\right)
=6\left(21+m-\frac7r\right).
\]

---

## 3. Range \([2/7,1/3)\)

Here \(r\in[2/5,1/2)\). Use

\[
d_2=28-m,\qquad d_3\le H_m,\qquad d_4\le17,\qquad d_5\le6.
\]

So

\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^8\bigl(1+8r+(28-m)r^2+H_m r^3+17r^4+6r^5\bigr).
\]

A finite polynomial check over \(m=0,\dots,28\) gives

\[
(1+r)^7-\bigl(1+8r+(28-m)r^2+H_m r^3+17r^4+6r^5\bigr)>0
\]

for all \(r\in[2/5,1/2)\). The worst case is \(m=0\) at \(r=2/5\), where the gap is

\[
\frac{1618}{78125}>0.
\]

Thus \(\mu_p(D)<q\) on \([2/7,1/3)\).

---

## 4. Middle range \([1/4,2/7]\)

Here \(r\in[1/3,2/5]\). Define the **very-high pair graph**

\[
E=\{ij:w_i+w_j\ge1-2p\}.
\]

If \(T,U\in D_3\) are disjoint, then their complementary pair belongs to \(E\). Also, if \(A\in D_5\), then every pair inside \(A^c\) belongs to \(E\).

For \(p\le2/7\), one checks:

\[
|E|\le6,
\]

and if \(|E|=6\), then \(E\cong K_4\). Hence:

- if \(|E|\le5\), then \(d_3\le31\) and \(d_5\le2\);
- if \(|E|=6\), then \(d_3\le28\) and \(d_5\le4\).

Combining this with the small-\(m\) blocker bounds for \(d_4\), the following table gives sufficient layer bounds. In each row, substituting the displayed bounds into

\[
(1+r)^7-\bigl(1+8r+d_2r^2+d_3r^3+d_4r^4+d_5r^5\bigr)
\]

gives a positive polynomial on \(r\in[1/3,2/5]\).

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c}
\text{case} & d_3 & d_4,d_5 & \text{minimum gap}\\
\hline
m=0,1,2 &
\min\{6(21+m-7/r),H_m\} &
d_4=d_5=0 &
>0
\\
m=3,4 &
\min\{6(21+m-7/r),H_m\} &
d_4\le5,\ d_5\le2 &
>0
\\
m=5 &
\min\{6(26-7/r),31\} &
d_4r^4+d_5r^5\le10r^4 &
>0.009
\\
m\ge6,\ |E|\le5 &
31 &
d_4\le17,\ d_5\le2 &
\frac{31}{2187}
\\
|E|=6 &
28 &
d_4\le17,\ d_5\le4 &
\frac{256}{2187}
\end{array}
\]

Therefore \(\mu_p(D)<q\) also on \([1/4,2/7]\).

Combining the three ranges proves:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No strict reduced counterexample of support }8\text{ exists for }1/5\le p<1/3.
}
\]

Together with the earlier \(n=8\) result on \([1/6,1/5]\), support \(8\) is now eliminated on the entire base interval \([1/6,1/3)\).

## Task t8_regular_critical_index — Develop a Berry-Esseen plus critical-index proof for large support.
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let

\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
Below is a **large-support / regularity progress report**. I did not close every nonregular configuration, but the complementary cases are now reduced to explicit finite-head certificates plus a critical-index decay lemma, and I also get one new small-support layer-counting extension.

Throughout, reduce to the strict case
\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1,\qquad p\in[1/6,1/3],
\]
since any \(w_i\ge p\) immediately gives \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\). Sort
\[
w_1\ge w_2\ge\cdots>0.
\]
For \(k\ge0\), write
\[
\alpha_k=\sum_{i\le k}w_i,\quad
B_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^2,\quad
C_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^3,\quad
\rho_k=\frac{C_k}{B_k^{3/2}},
\]
and
\[
\sigma_k^2=pqB_k,\qquad X_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i(v_i-p).
\]

Let
\[
K(p)=\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}.
\]
On \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\),
\[
K(p)\le K_*:=\frac{13}{3\sqrt5}<2.
\]

---

## 1. Finite-head / regular-tail Berry–Esseen certificate

For any fixed head size \(k\), condition on the head
\[
H_k=\sum_{i\le k}w_i v_i.
\]
Given a head set \(A\subseteq[k]\), the tail must satisfy
\[
X_k\ge p\alpha_k-w(A).
\]
Berry–Esseen gives
\[
\sup_x\left|\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_k}{\sigma_k}\le x\right]-\Phi(x)\right|
\le C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_k=:\varepsilon_k.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge
G_k-\varepsilon_k,
}
\]
where
\[
G_k=
\sum_{A\subseteq[k]}
p^{|A|}q^{k-|A|}
\overline\Phi\!\left(\frac{p\alpha_k-w(A)}{\sigma_k}\right),
\qquad \overline\Phi=1-\Phi.
\]

This is the main regular-tail certificate: if for some \(k\),
\[
G_k\ge p+C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_k,
\]
then \(B(p)\) holds.

---

## 2. A simple small-head corollary

Let \(C_{\rm BE}\le1\) for concreteness; otherwise replace constants by \(1/C_{\rm BE}\). Put
\[
\eta_0=\frac{1}{64C_{\rm BE}}.
\]
If for some \(k\),
\[
\rho_k\le \eta_0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\alpha_k\le \sigma_k,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]>p.
\]

Indeed, since \(H_k\ge0\),
\[
p\alpha_k-H_k\le p\alpha_k\le p\sigma_k\le \frac{\sigma_k}{3}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]
\ge \overline\Phi(1/3)-C_{\rm BE}K_*\eta_0
> \overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac1{32}.
\]
Numerically,
\[
\overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac13\approx0.0361>\frac1{32},
\]
so the right side is \(>p\) for every \(p\le1/3\).

Thus a genuinely regular tail after a head whose total mass is at most one tail standard deviation is enough.

---

## 3. One-dominant-head refinement

For \(k=1\), the finite-head certificate becomes especially clean. Let \(w=w_1\), \(\sigma=\sigma_1\), \(t=w/\sigma\). Then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]-p
\ge
q\overline\Phi(pt)-p\overline\Phi(qt)
-C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_1.
\]
Define
\[
M_p(t):=q\overline\Phi(pt)-p\overline\Phi(qt).
\]
Since \(q\ge p\) and \(pt\le qt\), one has \(M_p(t)>0\) for every finite \(t\). Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_1\le \frac{M_p(w_1/\sigma_1)}{C_{\rm BE}K(p)}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p.
}
\]

This handles many “one large atom plus regular tail” cases even when \(w_1\) is much larger than \(\sigma_1\). The remaining difficulty is that \(M_p(t)\) decays in the far tail, so absolute Berry–Esseen is not enough when \(t\) is very large; one would need relative moderate-deviation estimates there.

---

## 4. Critical-index decay for nonregular tails

Fix \(\tau>0\). If a tail is not \(\tau\)-regular, i.e.
\[
\rho_k>\tau,
\]
then, because \(C_k\le w_{k+1}B_k\),
\[
w_{k+1}>\tau\sqrt{B_k}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
B_{k+1}=B_k-w_{k+1}^2<(1-\tau^2)B_k.
\]

Thus before the first \(\tau\)-regular tail, the tail variance decays geometrically:
\[
B_{k+r}\le (1-\tau^2)^r B_k.
\]

Moreover, if no \(\tau\)-regular tail appears after \(K\), then the whole remaining tail mass satisfies
\[
\boxed{
\beta_K:=\sum_{i>K}w_i
\le
\frac{\tau}{1-\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}\sqrt{B_K}
<\frac{2}{\tau}\sqrt{B_K}.
}
\]

Proof sketch: put \(u_j=\sqrt{B_j}\). Since \(u_{j+1}\le \sqrt{1-\tau^2}\,u_j\),
\[
w_{j+1}=\sqrt{u_j^2-u_{j+1}^2}
\le
\sqrt{\frac{1+\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}{1-\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}}\,(u_j-u_{j+1}),
\]
and summing telescopes.

So a long nonregular segment forces geometric decay; either a regular tail eventually appears, or the remaining tail has small total mass and the problem becomes an explicit finite-head problem.

---

## 5. Deterministic tail-filling reduction

Suppose after a head of size \(K\), the remaining raw tail
\[
T_K=\sum_{i>K}w_i v_i
\]
has total mass \(\beta_K\). We always know
\[
0\le T_K\le \beta_K,\qquad \mathbb E T_K=p\beta_K.
\]
For any residual threshold \(r\), the sharp mean/range lower bound is
\[
\Pr[T_K\ge r]\ge \psi_{\beta_K}(r),
\]
where
\[
\psi_\beta(r)=
\begin{cases}
1, & r\le0,\\[2mm]
\dfrac{p\beta-r}{\beta-r}, & 0<r\le p\beta,\\[3mm]
0, & r>p\beta.
\end{cases}
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge
\sum_{A\subseteq[K]}
p^{|A|}q^{K-|A|}
\psi_{\beta_K}\bigl(p-w(A)\bigr).
}
\]

This is useful in the no-regular-tail case: critical-index decay gives an explicit small \(\beta_K\), and the last display reduces the problem to a finite weighted layer-counting check on the head.

---

## 6. New layer-counting extension: support \(9\) in the lowest base range

I can extend the previous support-\(8\) result one step.

### Proposition

If \(n=9\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac16\le p\le \frac15,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

### Proof

Let \(D_j\) be the losing \(j\)-sets:
\[
D_j=\{A\subseteq[9]: |A|=j,\ w(A)\le p\}.
\]

No losing set of size at least \(5\) exists, because a losing \(5\)-set plus the four remaining weights would have total
\[
< p+4p\le1,
\]
contradicting \(\sum_iw_i=1\).

For losing pairs, four disjoint losing pairs plus the ninth vertex would have total
\[
<4p+p\le1,
\]
so the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(3\). By Erdős–Gallai,
\[
|D_2|\le21.
\]

For losing triples, three disjoint losing triples would cover all vertices and have total at most
\[
3p<1,
\]
impossible. Thus the losing triple family has matching number at most \(2\). By the Erdős matching theorem for \(3\)-graphs on \(9\) vertices,
\[
|D_3|\le56.
\]

For losing \(4\)-sets, two disjoint losing \(4\)-sets plus the last vertex would have total
\[
<2p+p<1,
\]
impossible, so \(D_4\) is intersecting. By Erdős–Ko–Rado,
\[
|D_4|\le \binom83=56.
\]

Therefore
\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le
q^9+9pq^8+21p^2q^7+56p^3q^6+56p^4q^5.
\]
Define
\[
F_9(p)=
1-q^9-9pq^8-21p^2q^7-56p^3q^6-56p^4q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_9(1/6)=\frac{238705}{10077696}>0.
\]
Also
\[
F_9'(p)=
30pq^7-21p^2q^6+112p^3q^5+280p^4q^4-1.
\]
On \([1/6,1/5]\),
\[
30pq^7-21p^2q^6
=
pq^6(30-51p)
\ge
\frac15\left(\frac45\right)^6\cdot\frac{99}{5}
>1,
\]
so \(F_9'(p)>0\). Hence \(F_9(p)>0\) throughout \([1/6,1/5]\), proving the claim.

---

## Current state

The regular side is now quite explicit:

- If the whole vector is regular, Berry–Esseen proves \(B(p)\).
- If a regular tail remains after a small head, the small-head criterion proves \(B(p)\).
- If there is one dominant head atom and a regular tail, the one-head margin criterion often proves \(B(p)\).
- If no regular tail appears, the critical-index lemma forces geometric decay and reduces the problem to a finite-head layer-counting certificate with an explicit tail-filling function.

What remains is to close the finite-head certificates uniformly when the first regular tail occurs only after a large head, or when the no-regular-tail reduction leaves a large but geometrically decaying head. Absolute Berry–Esseen is too crude in the extreme-tail one-head regime; a relative moderate-deviation estimate for the regular tail looks like the next natural improvement.

I did **not** close the whole base interval \([1/6,1/3]\) in this pass. I did get several clean new reductions/lemmas that shrink the possible counterexample space, especially in the lower two subintervals.

Below \(q=1-p\), \(S=\sum_i w_i v_i\), and in the strict reduced regime
\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1,\qquad D=\{A:w(A)\le p\}.
\]

---

## 1. \(k\)-colour bootstrap lemma

Let \(B(r)\) denote the non-strict assertion at parameter \(r\). If \(B(kp)\) holds for an integer \(k\) with \(kp\le1\), then \(B(p)\) holds.

**Proof.** Generate \(\eta_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(kp)\). Conditional on \(\eta_i=1\), give \(i\) a uniformly random colour in \([k]\). For each colour \(j\), the indicators \(\mathbf 1_{\{i\text{ has colour }j\}}\) are iid Bernoulli\((p)\). Let
\[
S_j=\sum_{i:\,\text{colour }j}w_i,\qquad T=\sum_{j=1}^k S_j.
\]
If \(T\ge kp\), then some \(S_j\ge p\). Hence
\[
k\,\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge \Pr[T\ge kp]\ge kp,
\]
so \(\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge p\). ∎

Thus the global problem is indeed reduced to the base interval \([1/6,1/3]\).

---

## 2. New layer-counting progress

### Proposition 2.1: support \(7\), lower two ranges

If \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p
\]
for all
\[
\frac16\le p\le \frac14.
\]

#### Proof

Let \(D_j=\{A\in D:|A|=j\}\), and write \(d_j=|D_j|\).

---

### Case 1: \(\frac16\le p\le\frac15\)

No losing triple exists: if \(A\in D_3\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1,
\]
a contradiction. Hence \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge3\).

Also the losing-pair graph has no two disjoint edges: two disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining three vertices would have total weight
\[
<2p+3p\le1.
\]
Thus \(d_2\le6\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+6p^2q^5.
\]
So
\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge 1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5.
\]
Set
\[
F_1(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_1'(p)=30p q^4-1>0
\]
on \([1/6,1/5]\), and
\[
F_1(1/6)=\frac{4505}{46656}>0.
\]
So \(F_1(p)>0\), proving the claim in this range.

---

### Case 2: \(\frac15\le p\le\frac14\)

No losing \(4\)-set exists, because if \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+3p\le1.
\]
So \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge4\).

The losing triples form an intersecting \(3\)-uniform family: two disjoint losing triples plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<2p+p<1.
\]
By Erdős–Ko–Rado,
\[
d_3\le \binom62=15.
\]

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\): three disjoint losing pairs plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<3p+p\le1.
\]
By the Erdős–Gallai matching bound, \(d_2\le11\); I use the weaker bound \(d_2\le12\). Hence
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4.
\]
Let
\[
F_2(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_2'(p)=18p q^5+15p^2q^4+60p^3q^3-1>0
\]
on \([1/5,1/4]\), and
\[
F_2(1/5)=\frac{1316}{78125}>0.
\]
Thus \(F_2(p)>0\). ∎

---

## 3. Support \(8\) in the lowest base range

### Proposition 3.1

If \(n=8\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac16\le p\le\frac15,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### Proof

No losing \(4\)-set exists: if \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1.
\]
Thus \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge4\).

The losing triples are intersecting, since two disjoint losing triples plus the remaining two vertices have total weight
\[
<2p+2p<1.
\]
Hence
\[
d_3\le \binom72=21.
\]

The losing-pair graph has no matching of size \(3\): three disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining two vertices have total weight
\[
<3p+2p\le1.
\]
By Erdős–Gallai,
\[
d_2\le13.
\]
Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^8+8pq^7+13p^2q^6+21p^3q^5.
\]
Let
\[
F_8(p)=1-q^8-8pq^7-13p^2q^6-21p^3q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_8'(p)=30p q^6+15p^2q^5+105p^3q^4-1>0
\]
on \([1/6,1/5]\), and
\[
F_8(1/6)=\frac{115305}{1679616}>0.
\]
So \(F_8(p)>0\), proving the claim. ∎

Consequently, any strict counterexample in the lowest base range \([1/6,1/5]\) must have support at least \(9\).

---

## 4. Support \(7\), upper base range: matching obstruction isolated

### Proposition 4.1

Let \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac14\le p\le\frac13.
\]
If the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### Proof

Every set of size at least \(5\) is winning, because its complement has size at most \(2\), so
\[
w(A)>1-2p\ge p
\]
with strictness coming from \(w_i<p\).

The losing triples are intersecting: two disjoint losing triples plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<2p+p\le1.
\]
Hence
\[
d_3\le15.
\]

The losing \(4\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting: if two losing \(4\)-sets met in only one vertex, their union would be all seven vertices, and the sum of their weights would be at least \(1\), but also at most \(2p\le2/3\), impossible. Equivalently, their complements form an intersecting \(3\)-family, so
\[
d_4\le15.
\]

By assumption and Erdős–Gallai, \(d_2\le12\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4+15p^4q^3.
\]
Let
\[
F_3(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-15p^4q^3-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_3(1/4)=\frac{231}{8192}>0,
\]
and a direct derivative check gives
\[
F_3'(p)=18p q^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2-1>0
\]
on \([1/4,1/3]\). Hence \(F_3(p)>0\). ∎

So a support-\(7\) counterexample in \([1/4,1/3]\), if one exists, must have **three disjoint losing pairs**.

---

## 5. Useful degree test for arbitrary-support layer attacks

Let \(\mathcal H\subseteq D_k\) be any losing \(k\)-uniform subfamily. Let \(d_i=\deg_{\mathcal H}(i)\), and sort the degrees:
\[
d_{(1)}\le d_{(2)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}.
\]
Since every \(A\in\mathcal H\) satisfies \(w(A)\le p\),
\[
\sum_i d_i w_i=\sum_{A\in\mathcal H}w(A)\le p|\mathcal H|.
\]
Let \(s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\) and \(\theta=1-sp\). Since \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), the left side is at least the LP minimum
\[
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\theta d_{(s+1)}.
\]
Therefore every losing layer must satisfy the necessary condition
\[
\boxed{
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\bigl(1-sp\bigr)d_{(s+1)}
\le p|\mathcal H|.
}
\]
This is especially useful in the base ranges:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
p\text{-range} & s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\\
\hline
[1/4,1/3] & 3\text{ or }4\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/5,1/4] & 4\text{ or }5\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/6,1/5] & 5\text{ or }6\text{ at the endpoint}
\end{array}
\]

This degree test rules out many extremal-looking layer configurations that raw matching bounds allow. For example, in \(n=7\), an intersecting \(3\)-family of size \(15\) is a star, with degree sequence \((5,5,5,5,5,5,15)\). For \(p<1/3\), the degree test forbids all \(15\) triples from being losing.

---

## 6. Minimal-counterexample merge lemma

This is a useful structural condition for any future perturbation argument.

Assume \(w\) is a strict counterexample of minimal support. Let \(x=w_i\le y=w_j\) with
\[
x+y<p.
\]
Let \(R=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j}w_\ell v_\ell\). Merge \(x,y\) into a single weight \(x+y\). By minimality, the merged instance is not a counterexample. Comparing the split and merged probabilities gives
\[
\Pr_{\mathrm{split}}[S>p]-\Pr_{\mathrm{merged}}[S>p]
=
pq\Big(
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
-
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\Big).
\]
Since the split instance is bad and the merged instance is good, necessarily
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
<
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
}
\]
Thus every mergeable pair in a minimal counterexample forces more residual mass in the lower window \((p-x-y,p-y]\) than in the upper window \((p-x,p]\). This is a strong anti-smoothing condition.

---

## 7. Current remaining obstruction

After combining the prior support-\(\le6\) work with the new lemmas above:

- In \([1/6,1/5]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(9\).
- In \([1/5,1/4]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(8\).
- In \([1/4,1/3]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(7\); if support is \(7\), its losing-pair graph must contain three disjoint losing pairs.

The main unresolved piece is still an arbitrary-support layer bound that controls the tradeoff between many losing pairs and the forced scarcity of losing triples and losing higher sets.

---

## Task t7_merge_minimal_counterexample — Attack the minimal-counterexample merge obstruction.
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
Let \(q=1-p\), and for a set \(A\) write \(w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i\). I use
\[
D=\{A:w(A)\le p\},\qquad D_k=D\cap {[n]\choose k}.
\]

## 1. The naive universal good-merge statement is false

The statement “for arbitrary reduced weights there is always a mergeable pair with
\[
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]\ge \Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\]
” is false.

Take \(p=0.3\), \(n=7\), and \(w_i=1/7\) for all \(i\). Every pair is mergeable, since \(2/7<p\). For any pair \(x=y=1/7\), the residual is \(R=K/7\), \(K\sim \mathrm{Bin}(5,p)\). Then
\[
(p-x,p]=(0.157\ldots,0.3]
\]
contains exactly the residual level \(K=2\), while
\[
(p-x-y,p-y]=(0.014\ldots,0.157\ldots]
\]
contains exactly the residual level \(K=1\). Hence
\[
\frac{\Pr[p-x<R\le p]}{\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]}
=\frac{\binom52p^2q^3}{\binom51pq^4}
=\frac{2p}{q}<1
\]
for every \(p<1/3\). Thus **all mergeable pairs are bad** in the merge sense. But this vector is not a counterexample: for \(p=0.3\),
\[
\Pr[S>p]=\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(7,0.3)\ge 3]\approx 0.353>0.3.
\]

So the missing ingredient cannot be a purely local merge statement. One must use the actual counterexample hypothesis \(\mu_p(D)>1-p\) quantitatively.

---

## 2. A useful conditional good-merge lemma for the two smallest weights

Let \(a\le b\) be the two smallest weights, with \(a+b<p\), and let \(R\) be the residual sum. Define
\[
L=\{T:p-a-b<w(T)\le p-b\},
\]
\[
U=\{A:p-a<w(A)\le p\}.
\]
The merge is good exactly when \(\mu_p(U)\ge \mu_p(L)\).

For \(T\in L\), define its losing upper shadow
\[
\Gamma(T)=\{k\notin T:\ w(T)+w_k\le p\}.
\]
Because \(a,b\) are the two smallest weights, every residual weight is at least \(b\). Thus if \(T\in L\) and \(k\in \Gamma(T)\), then
\[
w(T)+w_k>p-a-b+b=p-a
\]
and also \(w(T)+w_k\le p\), so
\[
T\cup\{k\}\in U.
\]

Let \(K\) be the maximum number of lower-shadow predecessors of any \(A\in U\), i.e.
\[
K=\max_{A\in U}|\{k\in A:A\setminus\{k\}\in L\}|.
\]
Then
\[
\mu_p(U)\ge \frac{p}{qK}\sum_{T\in L}|\Gamma(T)|\,\mu_p(T).
\]

Therefore, if
\[
\frac{\sum_{T\in L}|\Gamma(T)|\,\mu_p(T)}{\mu_p(L)}
\ge \frac{q}{p}K,
\]
then the pair \(a,b\) is a good merge.

Equivalently, in a minimal counterexample where every mergeable pair is bad, the two-smallest lower window must have unusually poor \(p\)-biased upper-shadow expansion:
\[
\frac{\sum_{T\in L}|\Gamma(T)|\,\mu_p(T)}{\mu_p(L)}
< \frac{q}{p}K.
\]
For \(p\le 1/3\), the factor \(q/p\ge 2\), so this says the lower window must be very close to maximal losing sets. This is a concrete structural obstruction that any future proof must exploit.

---

## 3. Support \(7\), upper interval closed

I can close the remaining support-\(7\) upper-interval case, including the previous obstruction with three disjoint losing pairs.

### Proposition

Let \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac14\le p<\frac13.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

Thus no strict reduced counterexample of support \(7\) exists in the upper base interval.

### Proof

Let \(W\) be the graph of **winning pairs**:
\[
ij\in W \iff w_i+w_j>p.
\]
Write \(m=|W|\). Then
\[
d_2=|D_2|=21-m.
\]

Because every \(w_i<p\), every set of size at least \(5\) is winning:
\[
|A|\ge 5
\implies w(A)=1-w(A^c)>1-2p>p.
\]
So only layers \(0,1,2,3,4\) matter.

#### Step 1: losing triples are intersecting

If \(A,B\in D_3\) were disjoint, and \(z\) were the remaining vertex, then
\[
1=w(A)+w(B)+w_z<p+p+p=3p\le 1,
\]
a contradiction. Hence \(D_3\) is intersecting, so by EKR,
\[
d_3\le 15.
\]

#### Step 2: complements of losing triples force winning-pair blockers

Let \(T\in D_3\), and let \(Q=T^c\), so \(|Q|=4\). If \(Q\) could be partitioned into two losing pairs, then
\[
1=w(T)+w(P_1)+w(P_2)\le 3p<1,
\]
impossible. Hence \(W[Q]\) must meet every perfect matching of \(K_4\). A subgraph of \(K_4\) meeting all three perfect matchings contains at least three edges; its minimal blockers are a triangle or a \(3\)-edge star.

This gives the following graph-count bounds for \(d_3\):

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
m & d_3\text{ bound}\\
\hline
m\le 2 & d_3=0\\
3\le m\le 4 & d_3\le 4\\
m=5 & d_3\le 10\\
m\ge 6 & d_3\le 15
\end{array}
\]

The \(m=5\) bound is maximized by a \(5\)-edge star, which gives \(\binom53=10\) four-sets whose winning-pair graph contains a \(3\)-star.

#### Step 3: losing four-sets are \(3\)-intersecting

If \(A,B\in D_4\) and \(|A\cap B|\le 2\), then one can choose disjoint triples \(A'\subset A\), \(B'\subset B\). Since every subset of a losing set is losing, this would give two disjoint losing triples, impossible. Therefore \(D_4\) is \(3\)-intersecting.

Equivalently, the complements of sets in \(D_4\) form a \(2\)-intersecting \(3\)-uniform family on \(7\) vertices. Hence
\[
d_4\le 5.
\]
Moreover, if \(d_3\le 4\), then \(d_4\le 1\), because one losing \(4\)-set already contributes its four \(3\)-subsets to \(D_3\), and two distinct \(4\)-sets would contribute at least seven triples.

#### Step 4: pair-sum feasibility

The sum of all pair weights is
\[
\sum_{i<j}(w_i+w_j)=6.
\]
Each losing pair has weight at most \(p\), and each winning pair has weight less than \(2p\). Hence
\[
6\le (21-m)p+2mp=(21+m)p.
\]
In particular, when \(m\le 2\),
\[
p\ge \frac6{23}.
\]

#### Step 5: polynomial check

For bounds
\[
d_2\le a,\qquad d_3\le b,\qquad d_4\le c,
\]
we have
\[
\mu_p(D)
\le q^7+7pq^6+a p^2q^5+b p^3q^4+c p^4q^3.
\]
Writing \(r=p/q\), this is
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7(1+7r+a r^2+b r^3+c r^4).
\]
Since
\[
1-p=q=q^7(1+r)^6,
\]
it is enough to prove
\[
(1+r)^6>1+7r+a r^2+b r^3+c r^4.
\]

The four cases are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c}
\text{case} & (a,b,c) & r\text{ range} & \text{positive factor}\\
\hline
m\le 2 & (21,0,0) & r\ge 6/17 &
-1-6r+20r^2+15r^3+6r^4+r^5\\
3\le m\le 4 & (18,4,1) & r\ge 1/3 &
-1-3r+16r^2+14r^3+6r^4+r^5\\
m=5 & (16,10,5) & r\ge 1/3 &
-1-r+10r^2+10r^3+6r^4+r^5\\
m\ge 6 & (15,15,5) & r\ge 1/3 &
-1+5r^2+10r^3+6r^4+r^5
\end{array}
\]

In each row,
\[
(1+r)^6-(1+7r+a r^2+b r^3+c r^4)=r\cdot h(r),
\]
and \(h(r)\) is increasing on the stated interval. At the left endpoints the values are respectively
\[
\frac{187075}{17^5},\qquad
\frac{91}{243},\qquad
\frac{55}{243},\qquad
\frac1{243},
\]
all positive. Hence \(\mu_p(D)<1-p\), so
\[
\Pr[S>p]=1-\mu_p(D)>p.
\]
This proves the proposition. ∎

At \(p=1/3\), the original non-strict statement \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\) is already covered by the cyclic \(p=1/k\) argument with \(k=3\). The strict \(>\) endpoint is a boundary issue and is not needed for \(B(1/3)\).

---

## 4. Consequences

Combining this with the prior support-\(7\) result for \([1/6,1/4]\), support \(7\) is now eliminated throughout the base interval \(1/6\le p<1/3\). Therefore any strict reduced counterexample with \(p<1/3\) has support at least \(8\).

The merge program remains open in arbitrary support, but the two key takeaways are:

1. **All local merge inequalities can hold in non-counterexamples**, so the counterexample condition \(\mu_p(D)>1-p\) is essential.
2. In a minimal counterexample, every mergeable pair must have a lower-window family with abnormally small biased upper-shadow expansion.
